The Tracer Transport Module Part I Daniel Reinert
The Tracer Transport Module
Part I: A Mass Consistent Finite Volume Approach with
Fractional Steps
Daniel Reinert
Deutscher Wetterdienst
This document refers to release icon-2.6.0.
Abstract
The tracer transport module of ICON is presented and the consistency with the continuity
equation for air mass is discussed. The transport module solves the continuity equation
for tracer mass and, as such, describes the large-scale transport of gaseous, liquid or solid
atmospheric constituents due to air motion. Finite volume methods are applied and combined
with the fractional step approach (operator splitting) in order to derive mathematically
simpler subproblems for the horizontal and vertical directions. The mathematical derivation
of the discretized equation set is presented. Speciﬁc measures are taken in order to achieve
consistency with continuity (CWC). These include the reuse of time-averaged mass ﬂuxes
from the discretized air mass continuity equation, as well as the re-integration of the air mass
continuity equation as part of the fractional step approach. Results of an ICON forecast run
are presented in order to highlight that CWC is maintained, provided that the aforementioned
measures are taken.
Keywords: transport, continuity equation, ﬁnite volume, fractional steps, consistency with
continuity
Correspondence to: Daniel Reinert (daniel.reinert@dwd.de)
1. Introduction
The transport module is an important building block of any numerical weather prediction (NWP)
or climate model, as it predicts the large-scale redistribution of water substances, chemical
constituents or aerosols in the atmosphere due to air motion. Mathematically, it solves one of the
fundamental laws of physics, namely the equation of tracer mass continuity.
When developing a transport module for the atmosphere one is faced with several, partially
contradicting design objectives. One major challenge is posed by the contrasting requirements of
numerical accuracy on the one hand and computational eﬃciency (in terms of time-to-solution
and memory consumption) on the other hand. As the total number of prognostic tracers can reach
several dozen or even exceed 100 if atmospheric chemistry is taken into account, the transport
module can easily become one of the most time consuming parts of a NWP or climate model. A
Reports on ICON - Issue 5 3
The Tracer Transport Module Part I Daniel Reinert
pragmatic trade-oﬀ between accuracy and eﬃciency is thus unavoidable. On top of that there
exist many more important design objectives, such as high parallel eﬃciency, mass conservation,
consistency between tracer mass and air mass, or consistency among diﬀerent tracers. See e.g.
Lauritzen et al. (2011b) for an extended list.
Part I of this report series aims to present the underlying equations in continuous form and to
provide an introduction into the numerical discretization and solution procedure based on the
ﬁnite volume approach. We start in Section 2 with a description of the continuous form of the
tracer mass continuity equation and the connection to its nearest relative, the continuity equation
for air mass. Section 3 deals with the cell-integrated form, which forms the basis for ICON’s
ﬁnite volume discretization. This is followed in Section 4 by a description of the basic solution
procedure based on fractional steps. Details of the vertical and horizontal transport operators
will be presented in Part II and III of this report series.
2. The Tracer Mass Continuity Equation
In mathematical terms, the tracer mass continuity equation is a ﬁrst-order linear hyperbolic
partial diﬀerential equation with source terms. For an arbitrary species χ it reads in ﬂux form
∂ρqχ
∂t +∇· (ρqχ v) = Qρqχ , (1)
where ρ denotes the air density, v is the 3D velocity vector, t is the time,∇· represents a three-
dimensional ﬂux divergence, and qχ denotes the tracer mass fraction. In ICON, the prognostic
(moist) air density ρ is the sum of the dry air density ρd, the density of water vapour, and all
condensates. Hence, qχ is deﬁned wrt moist air density as follows
qχ = ρχ
